metadata.summary: Trello is a card-based kanban tool for small teams. Work items move through 5-7 named lists with WIP limits embedded in names. Butler automation handles mechanical card moves without custom code.

content.title: Trello Kanban
content.summary: Trello is a card-based kanban tool where work items move left-to-right through 5–7 named lists. Boards are the fastest PM setup for visual thinkers and non-technical stakeholders. Butler automation handles mechanical card moves without custom code. WIP limits are enforced by embedding them in list names (e.g., "In Progress [WIP:3]").

why.p: Trello's visual model eliminates the overhead of database schemas and custom fields required by heavier tools. For small teams with linear flows, the board IS the process — no configuration layer between the team and their work. Butler rules handle 90% of repetitive moves (auto-archive, auto-assign) as English natural-language rules, keeping automation accessible to non-developers.

list.item: Small teams (2–8 people) needing a visual board fast without field configuration
Non-technical stakeholders (marketing, ops, content) who find Jira or Linear intimidating
Projects with a simple linear flow: cards move left-to-right through defined stages
Butler automation covers most repetitive moves without custom code
Prototyping a new workflow before committing to a heavier tool
Engineering teams needing native Git/PR integration — Trello's GitHub Power-Up is shallow
Projects requiring sub-tasks, epics, or multi-level hierarchy — cards are flat; checklists are a poor substitute
Teams needing velocity tracking, cycle time analytics, or burndown charts without third-party Power-Ups
Large backlogs (500+ cards) — board performance degrades and visual scanning becomes impractical
Organizations requiring SSO, audit logs, or enterprise compliance — Enterprise plan only

section.name: board-setup
section.title: Trello Board Structure and Card Anatomy
section.p: Core patterns for structuring Trello boards: list naming with WIP limits, card anatomy, and label system.

rule.statement: Keep boards to 5–7 lists maximum; embed WIP limits directly in list names.
rule.rationale: Visual scanning degrades beyond 7 lists; embedding WIP limits ("In Progress [WIP:3]") makes limits visible to humans and queryable by agents without a separate config file.

example.note: Basic kanban: Backlog, To Do (This Sprint), In Progress [WIP:3], In Review [WIP:2], Done
Enhanced: Icebox, Backlog, Ready for Dev, In Development [WIP:3], Code Review [WIP:2], QA [WIP:2], Done, Archive

antipattern.description: Using 10+ lists to model every possible state.
antipattern.reason: Board becomes unreadable; WIP limits are meaningless across too many stages.

rule.statement: Use custom fields (Story Points, Sprint) instead of encoding metadata in card titles.
rule.rationale: Agents can query custom fields directly via API with customFieldItems=true; parsing metadata from titles requires fragile string matching.

rule.statement: One deliverable per card; use checklists for subtasks, not separate cards.
rule.rationale: Atomic cards keep WIP limits meaningful and flow metrics accurate.

rule.statement: Limit labels to 8–12; use one type label per card (Feature OR Bug, not both).
rule.rationale: Over-labeling creates noise; type exclusivity makes pie-chart reporting accurate.

example.note: Type labels: Feature (green), Bug (red), Tech Debt (orange), Docs (purple). Priority labels: P0-Critical (red), P1-High (orange), P2-Medium (yellow), P3-Low (blue). State labels: Blocked (black), Quick Win (lime).

antipattern.description: Creating labels per team member or per sprint.
antipattern.reason: Labels multiply unboundedly; use custom fields for sprint and assignee metadata instead.

rule.statement: Define custom fields: Story Points (number), Sprint (dropdown), Estimate (dropdown: XS/S/M/L/XL), Component (dropdown), PR Link (text).
rule.rationale: Custom fields enable agent queries and reporting without text parsing; dropdown fields prevent free-text variants.

section.name: automation
section.title: Trello Butler Automation and Power-Ups
section.p: Butler rules, board buttons, essential Power-Ups, and Trello REST API patterns for automation.

section.name: butler-automation
section.title: Butler Automation

rule.statement: Use Butler rules for mechanical card moves; use Butler buttons for triggered multi-step workflows that require human initiation (Start Work, Submit for Review, Archive Completed).
rule.rationale: Rules run automatically and should be reserved for deterministic transitions; buttons give humans control over multi-step state changes that should not run without intent.

example.note: Rule: "When all checklists on a card are completed → move card to Done." Button: "Start Work → move to In Development + add me + set due date 3 working days."
Using Butler to auto-move cards to Done based on due date passing — this archives incomplete work silently.

section.reference: Butler automation rule and button examples

rule.statement: Enable these Power-Ups in order of value: Butler (automation), Custom Fields (metadata), Card Aging (stale detection), Calendar (due date visualization). Add GitHub and Slack only if the team actively uses both.
rule.rationale: Free and Standard plans allow 1 Power-Up on free tier; prioritize automation and metadata over visualization. Card Aging surfaces stale work passively without a separate process.

rule.statement: Always request customFieldItems=true when fetching cards that need custom field values; resolve member IDs to names via GET /1/members/{id} before producing human-readable reports.
rule.rationale: The default card response omits custom field values; member IDs are UUIDs that agents must resolve to names for any report a human will read.

rule.statement: Archive cards via PUT /1/cards/{id}?closed=true; never delete cards.
rule.rationale: Archived cards remain queryable via GET /boards/{id}/cards?filter=closed for history and metrics; deleted cards are unrecoverable.

antipattern.description: Registering webhooks per card instead of per board.
antipattern.reason: Per-card webhooks must be re-registered whenever a card is copied or the board is recreated; board-level webhooks catch all card events in one stream.

section.name: agent-integration
section.title: Trello Agent Integration and Gotchas
section.p: Agentic workflow patterns, recommended subagents, API gotchas, and best practices for Claude agents using the Trello REST API.

rule.statement: Agents must request customFieldItems=true in card list queries and resolve member IDs to names before producing any human-readable output.
rule.rationale: Default card responses omit custom fields; member IDs (UUIDs) are meaningless to humans in reports. Both require extra API calls that must be built into the agent pipeline, not added ad-hoc.

rule.statement: Register webhooks at board level, not per-card; always compare incoming webhook payloads against cached state to determine what changed — Trello sends the full card object on every event, not a diff.
rule.rationale: Board-level webhooks survive card copies and board recreations; without diff comparison an agent cannot determine whether the label changed, the due date changed, or both.

rule.statement: Require human approval before bulk label changes or bulk archiving — there is no bulk undo in Trello.
rule.rationale: Bulk operations without human review are irreversible; a single bad filter can archive hundreds of active cards.

rule.statement: When an agent creates a card and immediately queries it, add a 2-second delay before the query — Butler rules run asynchronously and the card may not yet reflect applied labels or moves.
rule.rationale: Butler rule execution is async; the agent's read immediately after write may see the pre-Butler state, producing incorrect validation results.

rule.statement: Trello API rate limit is 300 requests per 10 seconds per token. When looping over 500+ cards with custom field fetches, implement batching with a 100ms delay between requests to stay under the limit.
rule.rationale: Each custom-field fetch is a separate request; 500 cards with custom fields = 1000+ requests. Hitting the limit causes 429 errors that break the pipeline mid-run.

section.name: recommended-subagents
section.title: Recommended Subagents

list.item: trello-intake-agent: triggered by board webhook on card creation; validates description completeness, applies labels from title keywords, posts comment with next steps.
trello-standup-agent: queries "In Progress" and "Blocked" lists daily; formats standup digest; posts to Slack.
trello-archive-agent: weekly job; archives cards in "Done" list older than 7 days.

section.reference: Prompt for agent to fetch and report in-progress cards

list.item: Define list names as verb phrases ("In Development", "Awaiting Review") so their meaning is unambiguous when agents filter by list name.
Store the Trello API key and user token in environment variables — never embed them in card descriptions or board names where they appear in webhook payloads.
Use custom fields (Story Points, Sprint) instead of encoding metadata in card titles — agents can query custom fields directly via API without text parsing.
Keep WIP limits explicit in list names ("In Progress [WIP:3]") so humans and agents can check limits without a separate config file.
Register webhooks at the board level, not per-card — fewer webhook registrations and you catch all card events in one stream.
Archive cards rather than deleting — archived cards are still queryable via GET /1/boards/{id}/cards?filter=closed for history.
Custom field values are NOT included in the default GET /1/lists/{id}/cards response — agents must request customFieldItems=true as a query param or fetch separately.
Trello API returns member IDs, not names — agents must resolve IDs via GET /1/members/{id} to produce human-readable reports.
Butler rules run asynchronously; an agent that creates a card and immediately queries it may see the pre-Butler state (e.g., no label yet applied).
Webhook model: Trello sends the entire card object on every change, not a diff — agents must compare against cached state to know what changed.
Rate limit (300 req/10s) looks generous but is per token; an agent looping over 500 cards with extra custom-field fetches can hit it quickly.
Human checkpoint required before bulk label changes or bulk archiving — there is no bulk undo in Trello.

template.purpose: Feature card description template with user story and acceptance criteria
Bug report card template with reproduction steps and severity
Butler automation rule examples: sprint flow, stale card alert, blocked escalation
Prompt for agent to fetch and report in-progress cards
